canonical_summary:
Bringing Into Use (BIU) is the moment an ITU-recorded satellite frequency assignment is treated as genuinely in service, and it is the single gating event that converts a paper filing into a protected entry in the Master International Frequency Register (MIFR). Under Article 11 of the Radio Regulations, the notifying administration has seven years from the Radiocommunication Bureau's receipt of the advance publication information or coordination request to bring the assignment into use, and Number 11.44B (added at WRC-12) defines that act as ninety continuous days of a capability-compliant space station at the notified orbital position.

For non-geostationary constellations, Resolution 35 (WRC-19) overlays a milestone schedule on top of the seven-year clock: 10 percent of the filed satellites must be deployed within two years after BIU, 50 percent within five years, and 100 percent within seven years. Falling short does not extinguish the filing outright but caps the recorded constellation at a multiple of what is actually flying, turning BIU from a binary event into a proportional, use-it-or-lose-it regime.

US-licensed operators interact with the ITU exclusively through the FCC's Space Bureau, which mirrors the ITU clock with Part 25 milestone certifications, suspension requests under Number 11.49, and administration-level correspondence with the Bureau.

ITU list of suspended satellite networks (No. 11.49)
FCC How to Prepare a USA ITU Filing
FCC Part 25 Space Station Licensing Process and Timeline
FCC FAQ: Processing of Space Station Applications
Space Intel Report: ITU rejects force majeure excuses

faqs.question: What does ITU Bringing Into Use (BIU) actually mean?
faqs.answer: Bringing Into Use is the regulatory event under Article 11 of the ITU Radio Regulations that converts a filed satellite frequency assignment into a recorded, protected entry in the Master International Frequency Register. For geostationary networks, Number 11.44B (added at WRC-12 in 2012) defines BIU as the deployment and continuous maintenance, for ninety days, of a space station at the notified orbital position with the capability of transmitting or receiving the assigned frequency. The notifying administration must inform the Radiocommunication Bureau within thirty days of the end of that window. Without a valid BIU before the seven-year deadline, the Bureau cancels the filing.

faqs.question: How does the ITU 7 year clock work?
faqs.answer: Number 11.44 of the Radio Regulations fixes the outer deadline. The Radiocommunication Bureau starts counting from the date it receives either the advance publication information under Section 9.1 or 9.2, or the coordination request under Section 9.6 or 9.30, depending on the band. The notifying administration has exactly seven years from that receipt date to bring the assignment into use and notify the Bureau. The Bureau sends a reminder no less than three months before expiry. If the seven-year window closes without a valid BIU notification, the entry is cancelled. Only the addition of genuinely new frequency bands resets the clock.

faqs.question: What is the 90-day continuous transmission rule?
faqs.answer: Before WRC-12, no formal definition existed and paper satellites proliferated. Number 11.44B now requires that a space station capable of transmitting or receiving the notified frequency assignment be deployed and maintained at the notified orbital position for a continuous period of ninety days. Both presence and capability must hold for the full window. A satellite that arrives at the slot but cannot operate on the notified band does not satisfy the test. The notifying administration must inform the Bureau within thirty days of the end of the ninety-day period, providing the actual BIU date used for the MIFR record.

faqs.question: How does the NGSO milestone rule from WRC-19 change BIU?
faqs.answer: Resolution 35 (WRC-19) overlays a deployment schedule on the standard seven-year clock for non-geostationary systems in the Ku, Ka, Q and V FSS, MSS, and BSS bands enumerated in the resolution. After initial BIU, the operator must deploy 10 percent of the filed constellation within two years, 50 percent within five years, and 100 percent within seven years. If the operator falls short, the recorded constellation is capped at a multiple of the actual fleet (ten times at year two, two times at year five, one times at year seven). BIU is therefore proportional rather than binary.

faqs.question: Can a leased or stop-gap satellite satisfy BIU?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided it meets every element of Number 11.44B. The leased asset must be physically positioned at the notified orbital longitude, must be capable of transmitting or receiving on the exact frequencies and in the notified band, and must remain there continuously for ninety days. Operators commonly relocate older satellites to defend slots, but the Radiocommunication Bureau looks at notified parameters and capability, not commercial ownership. A stop-gap that drifts off-station, operates on the wrong band, or loses transponder capability mid-window breaks continuity and invalidates the BIU.

faqs.question: What happens if a satellite fails after BIU?
faqs.answer: Number 11.49, as amended at WRC-12, allows suspension of the recorded assignment for up to three years. The notifying administration must inform the Bureau within six months of the suspension date; later notification shortens the available suspension period. The assignment must be brought back into use, again satisfying the ninety-day test under Number 11.44C, no later than three years from the suspension date. There is no fixed cap on the number of suspension cycles, but any gap longer than three years leads the Bureau to cancel the entry from the Master International Frequency Register.

faqs.question: Has the ITU actually cancelled BIU filings?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Radiocommunication Bureau deleted Iran's IRANSAT recordings in July 2011 after the suspension period exceeded the then two-year limit. The Radio Regulations Board has also suppressed Luxembourg's LUX-G3-4 assignments and addressed reinstatement requests for INTELSAT 157E. Force majeure relief under Resolution 49 is narrowly granted: Egypt obtained extensions on political and financial grounds, Iran was later granted a 2023 extension for IRANSAT-43.5E, but the Board rejected Luxembourg's LST/Cleosat request in 2022 and challenged the Papua New Guinea Intelsat filing at 50 degrees West, signalling that pandemic-era softness will not become a general waiver.

faqs.question: How do US operators handle BIU when only administrations can file?
faqs.answer: US-flag operators cannot deal directly with the ITU. The FCC's Space Bureau Satellite Licensing Division acts as the administration of record. Operators email SLD at fcc.gov to request a USASAT name and operating agency code, prepare SpaceCap and GIMS data, and submit a cover letter plus cost-recovery letter. The FCC then files with the Bureau, manages coordination correspondence, and submits the BIU notification once the operator certifies via the IBFS milestone process under 47 CFR 25.164 and 25.137. Suspension requests under Number 11.49 are also routed through SLD, which sends the formal letter to the Bureau on US letterhead.

faqs.question: What FCC Part 25 milestones map to ITU BIU?
faqs.answer: For geostationary networks, 47 CFR 25.164 requires launch and operation at the assigned orbital location within five years of grant, which is timed to allow ITU BIU before the seven-year Article 11 deadline. For non-geostationary systems, 50 percent of the maximum authorized constellation must be in orbit and operating within six years, and 100 percent within nine years. Non-US licensees seeking US market access are bound to the same milestones under Section 25.137(d). Within ten days of each milestone, the licensee files a milestone certification in IBFS; missing the deadline cancels the authorization and forfeits the surety bond posted under Section 25.165.

faqs.question: Does WRC-23 change the BIU regime?
faqs.answer: No, the WRC-23 Final Acts retained the existing framework. Article 11 Number 11.44, the ninety-day rule in Number 11.44B, the three-year suspension envelope in Number 11.49, and the Resolution 35 milestone regime for non-geostationary constellations all remain in force without loosening. The Radio Regulations Board's authority to grant extensions is still bound by Resolution 49 and limited to force majeure or co-passenger launch delay. WRC-23 confirmed the Bureau's mandate to suppress non-compliant filings, and recent decisions such as the Rivada Resolution 35 waiver illustrate that relief is narrow, evidence-driven, and conditioned on subsequent milestone compliance.

faqs.question: What is partial BIU and when does it apply?
faqs.answer: Partial BIU is the operative consequence of Resolution 35 (WRC-19) for non-geostationary constellations. Instead of a single binary BIU event, the recorded constellation size is progressively trimmed at each milestone to a multiple of the satellites actually deployed: ten times at year two, two times at year five, and one times at year seven. The notifying administration must submit modifications within ninety days after the milestone. If it ignores Bureau reminders, the Bureau modifies the entry to suppress orbital parameters of all undeployed satellites and stops considering those assignments in Number 9.36, 11.32, and 11.32A examinations.

faqs.question: How early should operators plan BIU activities?
faqs.answer: Most experienced operators back-plan from the Article 11.44 expiry. To leave room for in-orbit testing, drift to the notified longitude, and the thirty-day notification window after the ninety-day continuous-presence test, the qualifying spacecraft should be on-station and band-capable at least one hundred and twenty days before expiry. Launch contracts, propellant budgets, and FCC milestone certifications under Part 25 should be aligned with that target. For non-geostationary systems, manufacturing and launch cadence must also satisfy the Resolution 35 ten percent, fifty percent, and one hundred percent thresholds at years two, five, and seven after BIU.

h1: ITU Bringing Into Use (BIU): The 7-Year Clock, the 90-Day Rule, and Modern Enforcement
meta_description: ITU Bringing Into Use explained: the 7-year Article 11 clock, the 90-day rule under No. 11.44B, NGSO milestones under Resolution 35, and FCC handling.
meta_title: ITU Bringing Into Use: 7-Year Clock and 90-Day Rule

sections.heading: What BIU Is and Where It Sits in the Radio Regulations
paragraphs: Bringing Into Use is the regulatory act that converts a filed satellite frequency assignment into a recorded, protected entry in the ITU Master International Frequency Register (MIFR). It is the gating event for retaining priority on a notified orbital position and frequency band. Without a valid BIU within the deadlines fixed by Article 11 of the Radio Regulations, the Radiocommunication Bureau (BR) cancels the entry and the filing dies.

The BIU concept sits at the end of a three-stage lifecycle that the Radio Regulations impose on every space-service filing. Stage one is advance publication under Section 9.1 or 9.2. Stage two is coordination under Section 9.6 or 9.30, where applicable. Stage three is notification and recording under Article 11. The operative provisions for BIU are clustered in Article 11, Section II (Examination of notices and recording of frequency assignments in the Master Register), specifically Numbers 11.44, 11.44B, 11.44C, 11.44D, 11.44E, and the related suspension provision 11.49.

The Radio Regulations are a binding treaty instrument revised at each World Radiocommunication Conference (WRC). The current BIU regime reflects amendments adopted at WRC-12 (the 90-day rule and the three-year suspension window), WRC-15 (force majeure narrowing), WRC-19 (Resolution 35 milestones for NGSO constellations), and WRC-23 (which retained the existing framework without loosening it).

sections.heading: The 7-Year Clock Under Article 11 No. 11.44
paragraphs: Number 11.44 fixes the outer deadline. The notified BIU date shall be no later than seven years following the receipt by the BR of either the advance publication information under Section 9.1 or 9.2, or the coordination request under Section 9.6 or 9.30, whichever procedure applies to the band in question. The seven-year clock runs from the BR's stamped receipt date, not from any later operator milestone, and it is not paused by ongoing coordination disputes.

Number 9.1 establishes the upstream filing window: the API may be sent not earlier than seven years and preferably not later than two years before the planned date of bringing into use. Together, Sections 9.1 and 11.44 bound the regulatory lifecycle. If the seven-year window closes without a valid BIU notification on file, the BR cancels the entry. Consistent with general Article 11 practice, the BR sends a reminder not less than three months before expiry so the notifying administration can act.

The clock cannot be reset by mere amendments. Modifications to orbital parameters, antenna patterns, or carrier characteristics do not generate a new receipt date. Only the addition of genuinely new frequency bands creates a fresh receipt date, and only for those new bands. This is documented in Number 11.44.1 and reinforced by BR practice notes circulated through the WRS-20 briefing materials.

subsections.heading: What Starts the Clock
bullets: BR receipt of Section 9.1 or 9.2 advance publication information for filings outside the coordination procedure
BR receipt of Section 9.6 or 9.30 coordination request for filings inside the coordination procedure
A separate receipt date applies only to genuinely new frequency bands added later

sections.heading: The 90-Day Continuous Transmission Rule (No. 11.44B)
paragraphs: Before WRC-12, brought into use was not formally defined, which enabled the paper satellite abuse that peaked with Tongasat's 1990 filings for sixteen GSO slots. WRC-12 closed the loophole by adding Number 11.44B. For geostationary networks, an assignment is considered brought into use only when a space station in the geostationary-satellite orbit, with the capability of transmitting or receiving that frequency assignment, has been deployed and maintained at the notified orbital position for a continuous period of ninety days.

The capability test is strict. The spacecraft physically present at the notified longitude must be able to transmit or receive on the relevant band. Mere arrival without functional capability does not satisfy 11.44B. Operators using leased stop-gap satellites must therefore confirm that the leased asset matches the notified band plan, not just the notified longitude. Loss of transponder capability mid-window, drift off-station, or a switch to a different band breaks the continuous-presence requirement.

The notifying administration must inform the BR within thirty days from the end of the ninety-day window. The notification provides the actual BIU date that the BR will record in the MIFR. Because the BR also performs Number 11.31 examination on the notification, operators routinely target a BIU date at least sixty days before the 11.44 expiry to leave processing margin.

sections.heading: NGSO Milestone Regime Under Resolution 35 (WRC-19)
paragraphs: WRC-19 adopted Resolution 35 to address spectrum warehousing by mega-constellation operators. Under the prior Article 11 regime, an operator could bring frequency assignments into use by deploying a single satellite from a notified constellation of thousands and keep the full filing on the MIFR indefinitely. Resolution 35 ties continued recognition of recorded assignments to the actual count of in-orbit space stations against the filed total.

The Resolution applies only to NGSO frequency assignments in the bands enumerated in its Table, covering most of the Ku, Ka, Q and V FSS workhorses used by modern broadband constellations (10.7 to 12.75 GHz, 12.75 to 13.25 GHz, 13.75 to 14.5 GHz, 17.3 to 20.2 GHz, 27 to 30 GHz, 37.5 to 42.5 GHz, 47.2 to 50.2 GHz, and 50.4 to 51.4 GHz), with MSS and BSS sub-allocations. Bands outside the Table are not covered.

The three milestones are anchored to the end of the Article 11.44 regulatory period: Annex 1 deployment information is due no later than thirty days after the two-year, five-year, and seven-year marks following that end-date. If the operator falls short, Resolves 11 caps the recorded constellation at a multiple of the actual fleet. At year two, the recorded constellation cannot exceed ten times the number of deployed satellites. At year five, the multiplier drops to two. At year seven, the recorded constellation is reduced to exactly the deployed count.

subsections.heading: The Three Resolution 35 Milestones
bullets: Milestone 1 (year 2 after BIU): 10 percent of the filed constellation deployed, or recorded total capped at 10x deployed
Milestone 2 (year 5 after BIU): 50 percent deployed, or recorded total capped at 2x deployed
Milestone 3 (year 7 after BIU): 100 percent deployed, or recorded total capped at 1x deployed
100 percent is defined in Resolves 4 as every filed satellite, or every filed satellite minus one, to accommodate ordinary attrition

subsections.heading: Carve-Outs and Suspension Interactions
paragraphs: Resolves 12 lets WRC-23 or the Radio Regulations Board waive the milestone caps for filings whose 11.44 period ended before 28 November 2022, provided the administration shows by 1 March 2023 binding manufacturing and launch agreements (Annex 2). Resolves 9 (c) lets the BR credit peak in-period deployment when an operator can document attrition, and Resolves 9 (d) guards against double-counting the same satellites across multiple filings. Resolves 18 confirms that a Number 11.49 suspension does not pause the milestone clock, and Resolves 19 adds a post-completion duty: if the in-orbit fleet later drops below 95 percent (minus one) for six continuous months, the BR must be informed.

sections.heading: Suspensions, Cancellations, and Enforcement Cases
paragraphs: The companion suspension provision is Number 11.49. WRC-12 extended the maximum permissible suspension period from two to three years. The notification of suspension must reach the BR within six months of the suspension date; later notification shortens the available period. The date on which the recorded assignment is brought back into use shall be not later than three years from the date of suspension, after which the BR cancels the assignment under Number 11.44C. There is no fixed cap on the number of suspension cycles, provided each cycle complies with Section 11.49.

The Radio Regulations Board's force majeure jurisprudence under Resolution 49 (Rev. WRC-15) is narrow and uneven. Authorized grounds are limited to force majeure and co-passenger launch delay. In February 2015 the Board granted Egypt a three-year extension citing the 2011 to 2013 political and financial turmoil, a decision SpaceNews reported as precedent-setting because it was effectively a cash-flow argument. Egypt subsequently obtained a second extension. In 2023 the Board accepted that the sanctions situation around IRANSAT-43.5E qualified as force majeure and extended the bring-back-into-use deadline to 31 August 2024.

The Board has also rejected high-profile requests. In 2022 it declined Luxembourg's LST and Cleosat extension requests and pushed back on the Papua New Guinea Intelsat filing at 50 degrees West, formally asking why no replacement plan had been activated. Earlier enforcement included the July 2011 deletion of Iran's IRANSAT recordings after the suspension period exceeded the then two-year limit, and the suppression of Luxembourg's LUX-G3-4 assignments after a missed BIU deadline. WRC-15 reaffirmed the Board's limited mandate, declining to extend BIU windows for developing-country administrations whose difficulties did not fit the force majeure or co-passenger delay test. WRC-23 retained the regime without loosening enforcement.

For NGSO milestones, the most cited Resolution 35 waiver to date is the July 2023 decision on Liechtenstein's filing for Rivada Space Networks. The Board waived the 10 percent year-two milestone for the 600-satellite LEO constellation after the operator supplied the Annex 2 evidence on funding, manufacturing, and launch contracts. Rivada then had to meet the second milestone with 144 plus six spares by June 2026 and a further 144 plus six spares by September 2026.

sections.heading: FCC Handling for US Operators
paragraphs: US satellite operators cannot file at the ITU directly. Under Article 11, only an administration can submit advance publication information, coordination requests, and notifications, and only an administration can confirm BIU. For US-flag networks, that administration is the FCC, working through the Satellite Licensing Division (SLD) of the Space Bureau.

Before BIU is even a question, the operator originates a USA-prefixed ITU filing through the FCC. Per the Space Bureau's guidance, the operator emails SLD at fcc.gov to request a USASAT file name and an FCC-issued operating agency code, prepares the network data using ITU SpaceCap and GIMS software, and submits the SpaceCap database, GIMS graphics, an FCC ITU Filing Cover Letter, and the ITU Cost Recovery Letter back to SLD. The FCC then submits the package to the BR on the operator's behalf and manages all administration-to-administration correspondence with foreign administrations and the BR.

The FCC ties ITU BIU to its domestic Part 25 milestones. Under 47 CFR Section 25.164, GSO operators must launch and operate at the assigned orbital location within five years of grant. NGSO operators must have 50 percent of the maximum authorized constellation in orbit and operating within six years and 100 percent within nine years. Non-US licensees seeking US market access are bound to the same milestones under Section 25.137(d)(1) and (4). Within ten days after each milestone, the licensee files a milestone certification in IBFS and a Notice of Commencement of Operations. Missing the deadline cancels the authorization and forfeits the surety bond posted under Section 25.165.

Once the certification is on file, the FCC prepares the BR submission. The FCC's letter confirms BIU and, where relevant, attaches operator data so the BR can record the assignment in the MIFR. The FCC's FAQ on coordination confirms that the FCC uses draft responses from licensees to prepare a letter back to the ITU to bring the filing into good standing. For suspension under Number 11.49, the operator emails SLD with the filing name, location, and frequency bands, and the FCC sends the formal letter to the BR. For NGSO filings subject to Resolution 35, the FCC submits the equivalent milestone reports at year two, year five, and year seven through the same SLD mailbox.

sections.heading: Tactical Advice for Meeting the BIU Clock
paragraphs: BIU is a calendar problem first, an engineering problem second, and a regulatory problem only at the end. Most experienced operators back-plan from the Article 11.44 expiry rather than from launch readiness. The minimum critical path is ninety days of continuous capability-compliant presence at the notified longitude, plus thirty days for the administration to notify the BR, plus BR processing margin. Targeting a BIU completion at least one hundred and twenty days before the 11.44 expiry leaves room for in-orbit testing, drift to longitude, and notification.

Operators using leased or in-orbit stop-gap satellites must verify three elements continuously across the ninety-day window: physical presence at the notified longitude (not just nearby), transponder capability matching the notified band plan (not just the satellite's general band coverage), and uninterrupted operation across the full window. Any single-day gap on any of those three resets the count. Audit logs from spacecraft control should be retained because the BR may request supporting evidence.

subsections.heading: Practical Checklist
bullets: Identify the exact BR receipt date for the API or coordination request, in writing, and treat it as immovable
Back-plan launch and orbit-raising so a capable spacecraft is on-station at least 120 days before the 7-year expiry
Verify the leased or owned spacecraft is band-capable on the exact notified bands for every day of the 90-day window
File the BR notification within 30 days of the end of the 90-day window and ideally at least 60 days before expiry
For NGSO, align the production and launch cadence with the Resolution 35 year-2, year-5, and year-7 thresholds
For US operators, mirror the ITU dates in IBFS milestone certifications under 47 CFR 25.164 and 25.137
Pre-stage suspension requests under Number 11.49 if spacecraft anomaly risk is rising, since the six-month notification deadline is short
Document attrition events to preserve Resolves 9 (c) credit for peak in-period deployment

subsections.heading: Common Failure Modes
bullets: Treating the 7-year clock as starting from launch rather than from BR receipt of API or CR/C
Assuming amendments to orbital parameters reset the 11.44 clock (they do not, except for genuinely new bands)
Using a stop-gap satellite that is at the right longitude but lacks transponder capability on the notified bands
Filing the 11.44B notification more than 30 days after the end of the 90-day window, which the BR may reject
Treating Number 11.49 suspension as a deadline-free pause for NGSO filings (Resolution 35 milestones keep running per Resolves 18)
Relying on force majeure arguments that are essentially commercial or cash-flow problems, which the RRB has signalled it will reject
For US operators, missing the 10-day Part 25 milestone certification window in IBFS, which automatically cancels the authorization
